notes: Christian priest mentioned in letters by Cassiodorus and Ennodius of Pavia, noted for holiness and honorable character.
Roman figure described in Historia Augusta as considerate, eloquent, and skilled in verse; of noble bearing and some public life involvement.
Roman general known for his shrewd military leadership and temperate character, notably in the war against Perseus.
Spartan king known for his just and gentle nature, prominent in Plutarch's Lives.
Spartan king noted for his just nature and elevation of mind, praised by Plutarch.
Third grandson of Augustus, known for a coarse and unruly temper, adopted then discarded by Augustus.

person_name: Agrippina the Younger (mother of Nero, wife of Claudius)
person_name_full: Agrippina the Younger (mother of Nero, wife of Claudius)
notes: Mother of Emperor Nero and wife of Emperor Claudius, known for her political influence and ambition.

person_name: Alaric I, King of the Visigoths (r. 395-410)
person_name_full: Alaric I, King of the Visigoths (r. 395-410)
notes: King of the Visigoths who sacked Rome in 410 AD, noted for his leadership and interactions with Roman authorities.
A critic praised by Suetonius for being candid, just, and kind; associated with literary and poetic activity.
King of Macedon who created one of the largest empires of the ancient world through his military conquests.
Learned teacher mentioned by Libanius, described as kindly and youthful.

person_name: Amalasuntha, Queen of the Ostrogoths (regent, d. 535)
person_name_full: Amalasuntha, Queen of the Ostrogoths (regent, d. 535)
notes: Queen and regent of the Ostrogoths, known for her wisdom and political skill during the early 6th century.
King of Egypt known for wisdom and generosity, referenced in Roman-era letters as a figure of good fortune and counsel.
Lacedaemonian soldier known for bravery and desire to fight, mentioned in Plutarch's Lives.

person_name: Ananus ben Ananus, High Priest of Jerusalem (d. 68 CE)
person_name_full: Ananus ben Ananus, High Priest of Jerusalem (d. 68 CE)
notes: High Priest of Jerusalem, member of the Sadducees, noted for bold and reckless disposition around 68 CE.
Mentioned by Libanius, described with adjectives such as willing, skilled, calculating, harsh, wicked; likely a pagan individual of the 4th century CE.
Pre-Socratic Greek philosopher known for his cosmological theories, including the explanation of the moon's illumination.
Distinguished Christian man mentioned in letters of Cassiodorus and Pope Gregory the Great, involved in ecclesiastical matters in Ravenna.
Historical figure mentioned by Plutarch as a guide to Crassus, described as faithless.

person_name: Anicia Faltonia Proba, Roman Christian noblewoman (c. 360–c. 432)
person_name_full: Anicia Faltonia Proba, Roman Christian noblewoman (c. 360–c. 432)

Spartan diplomat known for negotiating peace with Persia, opponent of Agesilaus.
Antigonus was a powerful successor (Diadochi) of Alexander the Great who established a dynasty and took the title of king.
Philosopher of the Old Academy, known for favoring the original Platonic tradition over the New Academy.
Antipater, a Macedonian general and regent, known for his political and military role in the late 4th century BCE.

person_name: Antiphon of Rhamnus (Antiphon the Sophist, 5th century BCE orator and writer)
person_name_full: Antiphon of Rhamnus (Antiphon the Sophist, 5th century BCE orator and writer)

person_name_full: Antisthenes of Athens (Antisthenes the Cynic philosopher, c. 445–365 BCE)
notes: Athenian Cynic philosopher, pupil of Socrates, known for his asceticism and emphasis on virtue.

person_name: Antistius Sosianus, praetor and former tribune of the people under Nero
person_name_full: Antistius Sosianus, praetor and former tribune of the people under Nero
notes: Roman praetor and former tribune of the people under Emperor Nero, known for composing libellous verses against the emperor.
Roman Emperor from 138 to 161 CE, known for his just and frugal rule and being the adoptive father of Marcus Aurelius.
1st-century philosopher and miracle worker from Tyana, regarded as a sage and sometimes supernatural figure in pagan tradition.
Greek statesman and general, leader of the Achaean League in the 3rd century BCE.
A wise and just figure mentioned by Libanius and Plutarch, known for generosity and counsel; likely a notable elite or official in the 4th century CE.
Ancient Greek mathematician, physicist, engineer, inventor, and astronomer known for his contributions to geometry and mechanics.
Recipient of a letter from Libanius, a 4th-century rhetorician; described as gentle, moderate, noble, and kind.

person_name: Aristides of Athens, Christian apologist (fl. early 2nd century)
person_name_full: Aristides of Athens, Christian apologist (fl. early 2nd century)
notes: Early 2nd-century Christian apologist known for an apology addressed to Emperor Hadrian.
Known as 'the Just', referenced as a figure of integrity in Roman-era historical context, likely Aristides the Just, an Athenian statesman famed for fairness.
Tyrant associated with cruelty and viciousness during the Mithridatic conflicts, mentioned by Plutarch in the Life of Sulla.
Ancient Greek philosopher, student of Plato, tutor to Alexander the Great, foundational figure in Western philosophy.
Roman official known from Sidonius Apollinaris' letters, described as changeable, easygoing, loyal, and unlucky.
Christian consecrated virgin known from Jerome's letters, noted for her serious and quiet lifestyle.
Roman legal expert known for his knowledge of human and divine law, noted by Tacitus for a once brilliant public career and virtuous private life.
Bishop of Alexandria, champion of Nicene orthodoxy against Arianism.
Titus Pomponius Atticus, Roman friend and correspondent of Cicero, known for his careful and wise character.
Bishop of Hippo, influential Christian theologian and philosopher, author of Confessions and City of God.
First Roman emperor (r. 27 BCE–14 CE), established the principate and Pax Romana.

person_name: Aulus Vitellius (consul under Caligula and Claudius, father of Emperor Vitellius)
person_name_full: Aulus Vitellius (consul under Caligula and Claudius, father of Emperor Vitellius)
notes: Consul under Caligula and Claudius, father of Emperor Vitellius, known for viciousness and court favor.
Mother of Julius Caesar, known for her discretion and strictness.
Roman emperor from 270 to 275 CE, known for restoring the Roman Empire's unity and military strength.

person_name: Aureolus, commander of Gallienus' cavalry and usurper (d. 268)
person_name_full: Aureolus, commander of Gallienus' cavalry and usurper (d. 268)
notes: Commander of Gallienus' cavalry and usurper who rebelled during the Crisis of the Third Century.
Roman provincial governor praised by Isidore of Pelusium and mentioned by Jerome around 410 AD.
Christian Roman elite known for long public service and distinguished virtues, mentioned by Cassiodorus and Ennodius of Pavia.

person_name: Avitus of Vienne, Bishop of Vienne (c. 494–518)
person_name_full: Avitus of Vienne, Bishop of Vienne (c. 494–518)

Roman philosopher and statesman, author of The Consolation of Philosophy, executed by King Theoderic.
Spartan general noted for bravery, mentioned by Plutarch in context of Amphipolis and Spartan valor.
Praetorian Prefect Burrus, known for his strict discipline and influence over Emperor Nero's youth; mentioned in Tacitus and Historia Augusta.
Roman figure noted for blameless life and eloquence, mentioned by Tacitus in context of forum corruption.
Intimate friend of Scipio Africanus, known as 'the Wise' or 'the Prudent', involved in social reform efforts in late Roman Republic.
Roman general and statesman known for military reforms and multiple consulships during the late Republic.
A cruel and impious man mentioned by Plutarch, known for seizing treasure and killing a man to keep it secret.
4th-century pagan rhetorician praised by Libanius for his excellent reputation and character.
Cambyses II, king of Persia known for conquering Egypt and described as covetous and ferocious.

person_name: Capuanus, Vir Sublimis and judicial appointee under Theoderic the Great (fl. ca. 522)
person_name_full: Capuanus, Vir Sublimis and judicial appointee under Theoderic the Great (fl. ca. 522)

Roman emperor known for his cruelty and cunning, son of Septimius Severus, ruled 211-217 CE.

Roman military figure who led a rebellion and proclaimed himself emperor during the reign of Antoninus Pius.
Roman senator known for the Catilinarian conspiracy to overthrow the Republic; infamous for his ultraconservative and rebellious actions.
Roman statesman known for his stoic principles and opposition to Julius Caesar; famous for his inflexible temper and resolute character.
Athenian general known for his military skill and leadership in the 4th century BCE.
Semi-legendary Spartan king praised for his noble and just spirit, known as 'the joy of the people.'
King of the Sasanian Empire, often depicted as a bloodthirsty and depraved ruler in Procopius' Secret History.

person_name: Chromatius (Christian leader, correspondent of Basil and Gregory, 4th century)
person_name_full: Chromatius (Christian leader, correspondent of Basil and Gregory, 4th century)

Greek Stoic philosopher known for his contributions to logic and ethics, influential in Roman intellectual circles.
Roman statesman, orator, and philosopher known for his eloquence and political career during the late Republic.
Advisor and skilled orator for King Pyrrhus of Epirus, noted for his persuasive speech and diplomatic skill.

person_name: Claudia, daughter of Fulvia and Publius Claudius (first wife of Augustus)
person_name_full: Claudia, daughter of Fulvia and Publius Claudius (first wife of Augustus)
notes: Daughter of Fulvia and Publius Claudius, first wife of Augustus, noted for her purity and devotion.
King of Sparta, known for his leadership and reforms, contemporary of Agis.
Athenian political figure known for his boldness and wit, opponent of Nicias during the Peloponnesian War.
Military figure associated with Alexander the Great, known for his tragic death caused by the king's anger and intoxication.
Roman noblewoman known for her dissolute and wicked reputation, mentioned in Cicero's letters and Plutarch's biography.
Roman political figure known for violent and seditious behavior, described as an insolent demagogue hostile to Cicero.
Roman general and usurper who contested Severus for the imperial throne during the late 2nd century CE.
Roman emperor from 180 to 192 CE, known for cruelty and tyranny, son of Marcus Aurelius.
Roman Emperor from 337 to 350 CE, known for a harsh and vicious rule according to Zosimus.

person_name: Constantina Augusta, Empress (late 6th–early 7th c.), wife of Maurice
person_name_full: Constantina Augusta, Empress (late 6th–early 7th c.), wife of Maurice

Roman emperor who legalized Christianity with the Edict of Milan and convened the Council of Nicaea.
Roman Caesar from 351 to 354 CE, member of the Constantinian dynasty, noted for pride and ferocity.
Roman Emperor from 337 to 361 CE, son of Constantine the Great, known for his involvement in Christian imperial politics and religious controversies.
Roman general known for his conflict with the Roman people and alliance with the Volsci; subject of legendary accounts including those by Plutarch and Cassius Dio.
Cornelia, mother of the Gracchi brothers, noted for her noble and chaste character and her dignified mourning of her sons' deaths.
Roman poet and politician, known as the first prefect of Egypt and for his poetry; noted for an ungrateful and malicious temper in Suetonius.
Roman nobleman connected to Emperor Claudius by marriage to Antonia, involved in a conspiracy to raise him to the throne.
Bishop of Rome (pope) who died in 253, known for his firm and devout leadership in early Christianity.

Macedonian general under Alexander the Great, known for his strict adherence to Macedonian customs.
A devoted male figure mentioned by Libanius, related by marriage to Justinianus's family, active around 362 CE.
Bishop of Rome known for his eloquence and involvement in violent disputes over the bishopric.

person_name: Demetrias, great-grandmother of Demetrias (Christian noblewoman, 5th century)
person_name_full: Demetrias, great-grandmother of Demetrias (Christian noblewoman, 5th century)

Athenian statesman and orator famous for his Philippics against Philip II of Macedon.
Greek historian and philosopher from the Peloponnese, known for accurate historical accounts.
Blind scholar of Alexandria, renowned for mastery of liberal arts and Christian learning, visited by Antony the Great.

person_name: Diodorus of Tarsus, Bishop and Anti-Arian Theologian (fl. late 4th century)
person_name_full: Diodorus of Tarsus, Bishop and Anti-Arian Theologian (fl. late 4th century)

Christian bishop known for firm faith and pastoral leadership, mentioned by Eusebius and Pope Leo the Great.
Roman tribune known for proposing debt cancellation and association with Mark Antony.
Roman emperor from 81 to 96 CE, known for his authoritarian rule and persecution of Christians.

person_name: Domitius Afer, Roman prosecutor and orator (fl. early 1st century CE)
person_name_full: Domitius Afer, Roman prosecutor and orator (fl. early 1st century CE)

Presbyter of Antioch known for learning Hebrew Scriptures and being liberal and devoted in Christian faith.
Roman nobleman, son of Emperor Tiberius, noted for a dissolute and loose disposition.
Greek historian from Samos known for his careful and accurate historical writings, active in the 3rd century BCE.

person_name: Elagabalus (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus), Roman Emperor (r. 218–222)
person_name_full: Elagabalus (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus), Roman Emperor (r. 218–222)

person_name: Eusebia, Empress and wife of Constantius II
person_name_full: Eusebia, Empress and wife of Constantius II

Ascetic monk and bishop of Sebaste, early spiritual mentor and later theological opponent of Basil of Caesarea.
Commander of horse under Hicetes, noted for bravery and courage in Plutarch's account.
Prominent monk and abbot in Constantinople, associated with the heresy of Eutychianism (Monophysitism).
Bishop of Toulouse known for his charity and fasting, mentioned by Jerome.
Roman noblewoman known for penitence and founding a hospital at Portus; mentioned by Jerome.

Bishop of Riez in 5th-century Gaul, known as a learned and kindly literary churchman.
Roman nobleman mentioned by Tacitus, noted for mild and charitable disposition during political turmoil.

person_name: Florentius, Praetorian Prefect under Constantius II (pagan)
person_name_full: Florentius, Praetorian Prefect under Constantius II (pagan)

person_name_full: Fredegund, Queen of Neustria (wife of Chilperic I, d. 597)

person_name_full: Gaius Fabricius Luscinus, Roman consul and envoy to Pyrrhus (early 3rd century BCE)
notes: Roman consul and envoy known for his integrity and refusal to engage in treachery during the Pyrrhic War.

person_name_full: Gaius Mucius Scaevola, legendary Roman hero (early Republic)

person_name_full: Gaius Scribonius Curio (consul 76 BCE, general and politician of the late Roman Republic)

Roman military figure mentioned by Cassius Dio and Pliny the Younger, noted for boldness, temperance, and obstinacy.

person_name_full: George of Cappadocia, Arian Bishop of Alexandria (d. 361)

person_name_full: Gildo, Comes Africae (d. 398), rebel against Honorius

person_name: Gordian I (Marcus Antonius Gordianus Sempronianus Romanus), Roman Emperor (r. 238)
person_name_full: Gordian I (Marcus Antonius Gordianus Sempronianus Romanus), Roman Emperor (r. 238)

person_name_full: Gordian I, Roman Emperor (r. 238, the elder Gordian)

person_name_full: Gordian II, Roman Emperor (r. 238, co-emperor with Gordian I)

person_name_full: Heraclas of Alexandria, Bishop of Alexandria (early 3rd century)

person_name_full: Hierocles, imperial favorite and official under Elagabalus

person_name_full: Hilarius, palace official and soothsayer (associate of Patricius, 4th century, Ammianus Marcellinus) (christian)

person_name_full: Honoratus, Count of the East under Constantius II (fl. mid-4th century)

person_name_full: Hyginus, loyal official exiled by Maximus (late 4th c.) (christian)

person_name_full: James the Just, first Bishop of Jerusalem

person_name_full: Jesus Christ (the Saviour/Redeemer in Christian theology)

person_name_full: John, native of Palestine and chief of the treasury under Justinian

person_name_full: Jugurtha, King of Numidia (r. c. 118–104 BCE)

person_name: Julia Mamaea, mother of Severus Alexander, Roman Empress
person_name_full: Julia Mamaea, mother of Severus Alexander, Roman Empress
notes: Mother of Roman Emperor Severus Alexander, noted for her piety and religious life.

person_name_full: Julia the Younger (granddaughter of Augustus, daughter of Julia the Elder)

person_name: Julius Paulus, Roman jurist (early 3rd century CE)
person_name_full: Julius Paulus, Roman jurist (early 3rd century CE)

person_name_full: Liberius, Praetorian Prefect of Gaul and Patrician under Theoderic (fl. late 5th–early 6th c.)

person_name_full: Longinus (Cassius Longinus), philosopher and rhetorician (c. 213–273 CE)

Roman figure noted for loyalty to Antony at Philippi and mentioned for gracefulness in poetry.

person_name_full: Lucius Aemilius Paullus Macedonicus, Roman general and conqueror of Macedonia (consul 182 and 168 BCE, d. 160 BCE)

person_name_full: Lucius Quinctius Cincinnatus, Roman consul and dictator (fl. 5th century BCE)

person_name_full: Lucumo (later Lucius Tarquinius Priscus, fifth King of Rome)

person_name_full: Macrianus Minor (son of Macrianus Major, usurper under Gallienus, d. 261)

person_name: Marcion of Sinope (heresiarch, founder of Marcionism, fl. mid-2nd century CE)
person_name_full: Marcion of Sinope (heresiarch, founder of Marcionism, fl. mid-2nd century CE)
notes: Founder of Marcionism, considered a heresiarch; active mid-2nd century CE, originally from Pontus and a former Stoic student.
Roman general and five-time consul known for his skill and valor in war, first of his family called Marcellus.
Roman political figure noted for his ability and indifference, associated with Pompey and expected to become consul.

person_name: Marcus Octavius, Tribune of the Plebs (2nd c. BCE, opponent of Tiberius Gracchus)
person_name_full: Marcus Octavius, Tribune of the Plebs (2nd c. BCE, opponent of Tiberius Gracchus)
notes: Tribune of the Plebs in 2nd century BCE, known as an opponent of Tiberius Gracchus and described as steady and orderly.
Roman praetor noted for organizing seating for knights in the theater.

person_name: Marcus Terentius Varro, Roman scholar and writer (116–27 BCE)
person_name_full: Marcus Terentius Varro, Roman scholar and writer (116–27 BCE)
notes: Roman scholar and writer, considered the most learned of the Latin grammarians in his time.
Roman of provincial descent from Cales, from a family of consuls and knights, noted for amiable temper and cultivated eloquence.
Military tribune mentioned by Ammianus Marcellinus, noted for intemperate disposition and suicide at Aquileia.

person_name: Marius Victorinus, Roman philosopher and Christian convert (4th century)
person_name_full: Marius Victorinus, Roman philosopher and Christian convert (4th century)
notes: Roman rhetorician and philosopher, translator of Plotinus, who converted to Christianity in the 4th century.
Roman general and politician, ally of Julius Caesar and rival of Octavian, key figure in the transformation of the Roman Republic into the Roman Empire.
Christian correspondent praised by Basil of Caesarea and Isidore of Pelusium for cultured and remarkable mind.
Roman emperor (r. 306-312), son of Maximian, known for persecuting Christians and opposing Constantine.
Roman emperor and co-ruler with Diocletian, known as Maximian Herculius.
Christian bishop known from letters of Avitus of Vienne and Cassiodorus, active in early 6th century.

person_name: Maximinus (the young man mentioned in Historia Augusta, ha_maximini_duo)
person_name_full: Maximinus (the young man mentioned in Historia Augusta, ha_maximini_duo)
notes: Young man mentioned in the Historia Augusta, described as handsome and learned in Greek culture, associated with Maximinus.
Roman emperor from 235 to 238 CE, known for his barbarian origins and military career.
Christian bishop known from correspondence with Augustine of Hippo and Avitus of Vienne around late 5th to early 6th century.

person_name: Maximus, philosopher and associate of Emperor Julian
person_name_full: Maximus, philosopher and associate of Emperor Julian
notes: Philosopher and teacher of Emperor Julian, known for his learning and eloquence.
Physician known for success in treating desperate diseases, nicknamed 'Jupiter' due to vanity; mentioned in context with King Agesilaus.
Roman consul noted for military command and desire for triumph; mentioned by Cicero and Cassius Dio.
Athenian general known for leadership at the Battle of Marathon, praised for wisdom and prudence.
Roman general known for his boldness and eagerness in military action during the time of Fabius.
Noble and honorable individual addressed by Libanius, likely a rhetorician or public figure in a Roman city.

person_name: Mummius, Roman general (conqueror of Corinth, fl. 2nd century BCE)
person_name_full: Mummius, Roman general (conqueror of Corinth, fl. 2nd century BCE)
notes: Roman general known for conquering Corinth in the 2nd century BCE.
Competent and popular figure mentioned by Ammianus Marcellinus, tasked with explaining sects like the Manicheans; originally named Strategius.
Carthaginian cavalry commander under Hanno and Hannibal, noted for his valor and later defection to the Romans.
Tyrant of the Lacedaemonians (Sparta), known for profligate and lawless rule, opposed by Titus.

person_name: Nebridius (husband of Olympias, late 4th century nobleman)
person_name_full: Nebridius (husband of Olympias, late 4th century nobleman)
notes: Late 4th century nobleman, husband of Olympias, noted for high rank and irreproachable character.
A pagan individual known from Augustine's and Basil's letters, noted for his frank admission of paganism and respectful appeal to Christian mercy in late antique North Africa.
Macedonian military figure known for pride and violence, involved in frontier affairs in Cilicia and Armenia.

person_name: Nepotian, presbyter of Altinum (nephew of Heliodorus)
person_name_full: Nepotian, presbyter of Altinum (nephew of Heliodorus)
notes: Presbyter of Altinum, nephew of Bishop Heliodorus, known from Jerome's letters.
Christian priest of Limoges around 490 AD, praised by Ruricius for his holiness and devotion.
Roman emperor known for his controversial reign and persecution of Christians, including the Apostle Paul.
Roman emperor (r. 96-98 CE) known for his honorable and strict character, praised by Pliny the Younger.
Bishop of Trier around 550 AD, known for opposing Arianism among Germanic peoples.
Loyal figure associated with Pyrrhus' time, known for offering to assassinate King Pyrrhus; mentioned in Roman historical sources.

person_name: Numenius of Apamea (Pythagorean philosopher, 2nd century CE)
person_name_full: Numenius of Apamea (Pythagorean philosopher, 2nd century CE)
notes: Pythagorean philosopher from Apamea, known for his eloquence and impartiality, active in the 2nd century CE.
Roman emperor (r. 283-284), son of Carus, noted for his eloquence and leadership during the Crisis of the Third Century.
Sister of Emperor Augustus, known for her virtue and connection to the Julio-Claudian dynasty.
Roman prefect noted for his careful, kind, and humane administration.
Christian deaconess known for her austere and inflexible character, associated with John Chrysostom and imperial conflicts.
A valiant military officer under Aristides, involved in defense of Megarians.
Early Christian theologian and philosopher known for his extensive biblical exegesis and influence on later Christian thought; considered brilliant but later deemed heretical by some.
Roman emperor in 69 CE during the Year of the Four Emperors, known for his brief reign and association with Poppaea Sabina.
Roman individual mentioned by Cicero, described as cautious and of good character.
Roman military figure mentioned by Cicero, noted for his high character and courage.

person_name: Palladius, Master of the Ceremonies under Constantius Gallus (4th century) (pagan)
person_name_full: Palladius, Master of the Ceremonies under Constantius Gallus (4th century) (pagan)

notes: Roman Christian nobleman known for his devotion and correspondence with Church Fathers such as Augustine and Jerome; recognized as a saint.
A man praised by Libanius for his firm and honest character, known for loyalty and wise counsel.

person_name: Patiens of Lyon, Bishop of Lyon (5th century)
person_name_full: Patiens of Lyon, Bishop of Lyon (5th century)
notes: Bishop of Lyon in the 5th century, noted for holiness, energy, strictness, mercy, and generosity toward the poor.
Trusted advisor and friend of Seleucus, known for his valued judgment in political matters.
Apostle Paul, early Christian missionary and author of several New Testament epistles, known for his conversion and preaching.
Distinguished and humble Christian servant of God mentioned by Augustine and Cassiodorus, noted for renouncing worldly honors for Christ.
Wealthy Roman aristocrat turned ascetic and bishop of Nola, admired Christian figure and correspondent of Augustine and Jerome.
Spartan general known for his role in the Greco-Persian Wars, notably the Battle of Plataea.

person_name: Pelagius, British Monk and Theologian (fl. late 4th–early 5th c.)
person_name_full: Pelagius, British Monk and Theologian (fl. late 4th–early 5th c.)
notes: British monk and theologian known for teachings on human will and grace, active late 4th to early 5th century.
Theban general known for his role in the victory at Leuctra against the Spartans.

person_name: Peregrinus Proteus, Cynic philosopher (d. ca. 165 CE)
person_name_full: Peregrinus Proteus, Cynic philosopher (d. ca. 165 CE)
notes: Cynic philosopher known for self-immolation at the Olympic Games around 165 CE.
Athenian statesman known for his leadership during Athens' Golden Age and his oratory skills.
Roman satirical poet known for his virtuous and modest character, author of six satires.
Roman emperor in 193 CE, known for his sternness and brief reign before assassination.

person_name: Peter the Apostle (Saint Peter, first Bishop of Rome)
person_name_full: Peter the Apostle (Saint Peter, first Bishop of Rome)
notes: One of the twelve apostles of Jesus, traditionally considered the first Bishop of Rome and a foundational figure in early Christianity.
Distinguished Christian nobleman and count, mentioned in letters involving Pope Simplicius and Emperor Zeno.
Persian satrap and military commander, known for his zealous support of the Lacedaemonians during the Peloponnesian War.
Learned and experienced man recalled from banishment to counterbalance Plato's influence in Sicily.
Son of Parmenio, a general and close companion of Alexander the Great; involved in military and political affairs during Alexander's reign.
Byzantine emperor known for a reign marked by integrity and honesty, mentioned by Procopius and Pope Gregory the Great.
Athenian statesman and general known for his integrity and philosophical education under Plato and Xenocrates.
Son of Antonina, known for jealousy and spite, mentioned in Procopius' Secret History.
Athenian tyrant known for his noble lineage and political influence in 6th century BCE Athens.
Roman prefect under Emperor Severus, noted for tyrannical behavior and pride, mentioned by Ammianus Marcellinus.

person_name: Polemon of Athens, head of the Academy and successor of Xenocrates (Academic philosopher, c. 350–270 BCE)
person_name_full: Polemon of Athens, head of the Academy and successor of Xenocrates (Academic philosopher, c. 350–270 BCE)
notes: Head of the Academy in Athens, successor of Xenocrates, known as an Academic philosopher.
Bishop of Smyrna, early Christian martyr known for his steadfast faith and confrontation with Roman authorities.
Pope of the early 5th century, known for correspondence with John Chrysostom.
Wife of Brutus, known for her understanding and courage, mentioned in Plutarch's Lives.
Roman usurper emperor of the Gallic Empire (r. 260-269), known for establishing independent rule in Gaul and claiming titles such as Restitutor Orbis.
Christian individual addressed by Sidonius Apollinaris, noted for being a devoted friend.

person_name: Priscus of Epirus, Neoplatonist philosopher and friend of Julian the Apostate
person_name_full: Priscus of Epirus, Neoplatonist philosopher and friend of Julian the Apostate
notes: Neoplatonist philosopher from Epirus, friend of Emperor Julian the Apostate.
Roman secretary and nobleman involved in a revolutionary movement; guardian of a Christian woman related to Gregory of Nazianzus.

person_name: Proculus, Roman jurist or official (2nd century CE, contemporary of Fronto)
person_name_full: Proculus, Roman jurist or official (2nd century CE, contemporary of Fronto)
notes: Roman jurist or official in the 2nd century CE, contemporary of Fronto, noted for severity in passing sentence but otherwise easy-going.

person_name: Prosper, Master of the Horse in Gaul under Constantius II (pagan)
person_name_full: Prosper, Master of the Horse in Gaul under Constantius II (pagan)

person_name: Prusias II of Bithynia (king, r. 182–149 BCE)
person_name_full: Prusias II of Bithynia (king, r. 182–149 BCE)
notes: King of Bithynia, reigned 182–149 BCE, described as irritable and involved in political affairs with Rome.
Roman figure named Publius, associated with Cicero and Crassus, likely a member of the Roman elite in late Republic.
Roman nobleman, brother to Lucullus's wife, known for insolence and vicious behavior, opponent of Lucullus.

person_name: Publius Cornelius Lentulus Spinther, Roman consul and governor (consul 57 BCE, 1st century BCE)
person_name_full: Publius Cornelius Lentulus Spinther, Roman consul and governor (consul 57 BCE, 1st century BCE)
notes: Roman consul in 57 BCE and governor, involved in political maneuvers supporting Pompey.
Ancient Greek philosopher and founder of Pythagoreanism, known for religious and mystical teachings and mathematical contributions.
Roman consul in 95 BCE, distinguished governor of Asia, famous jurist and teacher of Cicero.
Roman emperor briefly in 270 CE, known for being unwarlike and committed suicide after army favored Aurelian.
A quiet, peaceable Roman man mentioned in Plutarch's Life of Sulla, known for being proscribed during Sulla's proscriptions.
Roman general known for military campaigns in the late Republic; contemptuous in battle as per Cassius Dio.
Roman orator and advocate, known for his eloquence and rivalry with Cicero.
Roman nobleman, son of Lucius Metellus, noted for courage and firmness, experienced exile and return.
Roman figure mentioned by Tacitus as accusing Sancia, described as restless and seeking favor with the sovereign.

person_name: Quintus Serenus, author of medical prescriptions in hexameter (possibly Serenus Sammonicus)
person_name_full: Quintus Serenus, author of medical prescriptions in hexameter (possibly Serenus Sammonicus)
notes: Author of medical prescriptions in hexameter, possibly identified with Serenus Sammonicus; known for kindness and generosity.
Official titled 'master of the offices' mentioned by Ammianus Marcellinus, involved in political-military affairs in late Roman Empire.
Roman nobleman of the Julian family known for austere manners and seclusion, mentioned by Tacitus.

person_name: Saturninus, Roman military official (fl. 4th century, in Ammianus Marcellinus and Historia Augusta)
person_name_full: Saturninus, Roman military official (fl. 4th century, in Ammianus Marcellinus and Historia Augusta)
notes: Roman military official active in the 4th century, mentioned in Ammianus Marcellinus and Historia Augusta.
Friend of Cicero, associated with Epicurean philosophy, mentioned in Cicero's letters.
Roman general known for the destruction of Carthage and Numantia; called indomitable for his vigilance.
Roman general famed for defeating Hannibal in the Second Punic War, known as Scipio Africanus.
Bishop addressed by Pope Gregory the Great around 594 AD regarding church discipline.
Lucius Aelius Sejanus, ambitious prefect under Emperor Tiberius, known for his political intrigue and eventual downfall.
Roman Stoic philosopher, statesman, and rhetorician known for his eloquence and moral essays.
Queen of Palmyra who ruled 267–272 CE, known for her bravery and leadership in the East against Rome and Persia.
Roman scholar and physician, tutor and friend of the family of Emperor Gordian III, known for his literary and medical works.
A figure mentioned by Procopius as a treacherous individual who broke an oath and killed ambassadors; described negatively in historical context.
Corrupt Roman official known for collaboration with the Visigoths, mentioned by Sidonius Apollinaris.
Roman general and statesman who led a significant resistance in Spain during the late Republic, known for his bold and warlike character.

person_name: Severus, Roman general (4th century, commander at Rheims)
person_name_full: Severus, Roman general (4th century, commander at Rheims)
notes: Roman general commanding at Rheims in the 4th century, noted for military skill and discipline.
Roman figure known for destroying prominent men of Gabii and distributing their wealth; mentioned in Cicero's letters and Cassius Dio's history.
Ancient Greek lyric poet known for his virtuous character and thoughtful deliberation on the nature of the gods.
Christian bishop of Milan, successor to Ambrose, known for his learning and role in the conversion of Marius Victorinus.
Christian figure addressed by Augustine and Sidonius Apollinaris, noted for eagerness to understand Christian teachings.
Classical Athenian philosopher, known for his method of inquiry and influence on Western philosophy.
Ancient Greek tragedian, author of classic plays such as Oedipus Rex and Antigone.
Thracian gladiator and leader of a major slave revolt against Rome (Third Servile War).
Lacedaemonian military figure and governor in Thespiae, known for boldness and involvement in factional conflicts during the 4th century BCE.
An audacious and impertinent figure mentioned by Plutarch, noted for buffoonery and familiarity with the people, possibly a political figure in ancient Greece.
Roman general and dictator known for his civil war against Marius and proscriptions.
Bishop of Bourges in the 7th century, recipient of a letter from Desiderius of Cahors.
Christian ascetic and writer, known for his hagiography of Saint Martin of Tours and correspondence with Paulinus of Nola.
A learned and gifted Christian figure praised by Pope Gregory the Great and Sidonius Apollinaris, noted for charity and refined taste; likely a Roman elite of the late 5th century.
Distinguished pagan senator and orator, served as prefect of the city during the late 4th century, known for petitioning Emperor Valentinian II.
Last king of Rome, known for his tyranny and exile, traditionally dated to the late 6th century BCE.

person_name: Tauriscus, legendary tyrant of Spain defeated by Hercules
person_name_full: Tauriscus, legendary tyrant of Spain defeated by Hercules
notes: Legendary tyrant of Spain defeated by Hercules, described as cruel.
Wife of Cicero, known for her involvement in his public affairs and her strong character.
Roman philosopher and orator known for his eloquence and service at the imperial court.
Athenian general and statesman, famous for his role in the Greek victory at the Battle of Salamis during the Persian Wars.

person_name: Theodora, Empress of the Eastern Roman Empire (wife of Justinian I, r. 527–548)
person_name_full: Theodora, Empress of the Eastern Roman Empire (wife of Justinian I, r. 527–548)
notes: Empress of the Eastern Roman Empire, wife of Emperor Justinian I (r. 527–548), influential in politics and religious affairs.

person_name: Theodoric the Great, King of the Ostrogoths (r. 471–526)
person_name_full: Theodoric the Great, King of the Ostrogoths (r. 471–526)
notes: King of the Ostrogoths (r. 471–526), ruler of Italy, known for maintaining Roman traditions and Christian faith.
A rhetorician mentioned by Libanius, noted for his skill in law and oratory.

person_name: Theodorus, senior imperial secretary (notarius) under Constantius II and Julian
person_name_full: Theodorus, senior imperial secretary (notarius) under Constantius II and Julian
notes: Senior imperial secretary (notarius) under emperors Constantius II and Julian, noted for his distinguished and noble character.
Roman Emperor from 402 to 450, noted for his piety and involvement in church affairs.
Chief magistrate of Hierapolis known for political dealings during the reign of Constantius and Julian; described as flattering and deceitful.

person_name: Theophilus (Ammianus, accused of treason, 4th century Roman official)
person_name_full: Theophilus (Ammianus, accused of treason, 4th century Roman official)
notes: 4th century Roman official, governor of Syria, mentioned by Ammianus Marcellinus and others, accused of treason.
Greek philosopher, successor to Aristotle as head of the Peripatetic school, known for his works on ethics and natural philosophy.
Queen of the Lombards, noted for her Catholic faith and diplomatic relations with Pope Gregory the Great.
Athenian general and political figure, known for opposition to Alcibiades during the Peloponnesian War.
Freedman of Antony, known for conveying messages between Antony and Cleopatra.
Roman emperor from 14 to 37 CE, known for his cautious and sometimes cruel rule.
Grandson of Emperor Tiberius, known for his excitable temperament and involvement in imperial succession intrigues.
Roman politician and reformer known for his agrarian reforms and tragic death; brother of Gaius Gracchus.
Powerful and profligate favorite of Emperor Nero, known for lewd and wicked behavior.
Greek statesman and general known for his opposition to tyranny and leadership in Sicily.
Brother of Timoleon, described as indiscreet and rash, involved with foreign soldiers and power struggles.
Tiro was a trusted and honest young man associated with Cicero, known for his modesty and trustworthiness.
Roman elite known from letters of Libanius and Symmachus, associated with rhetoric and traditional Roman religious observance.

person_name: Titus Quinctius Flamininus, Roman general and consul (c. 229–174 BCE)
person_name_full: Titus Quinctius Flamininus, Roman general and consul (c. 229–174 BCE)
notes: Roman general and consul known for his moderate and kind temperament, active in the 3rd century BCE.
Christian priest mentioned by Gregory the Great; also referenced as a skilled soldier by Ammianus Marcellinus.
Roman Emperor from 364 to 378 CE, known for supporting Arian Christianity and his role in the Gothic War, died at the Battle of Adrianople.
Roman Emperor from 375 to 392, involved in Christian religious affairs and opposed Arianism.
Christian bishop named Valerianus, known from letters by Avitus of Vienne and Basil of Caesarea, noted for his devotion and genuine love for the early church.
Roman man of high rank, influential and skillful in discerning and promoting excellence, mentioned by Plutarch.
Roman consul nominated by Galba, known for his candid and gentle temperament, and for writing about the civil war after Julius Caesar's death.
Bishop of Hippo in the late 4th century, predecessor and contemporary of Augustine of Hippo.
Roman political figure known for rude and insolent behavior in court, mentioned in Cicero's writings and Plutarch's Lives.

person_name: Vettius Agorius Praetextatus, Roman senator and governor of Achaia (d. 384)
person_name_full: Vettius Agorius Praetextatus, Roman senator and governor of Achaia (d. 384)
notes: Roman senator and governor of Achaia, noted for noble and old-fashioned dignity, active during the reign of Julian.

person_name: Victricius, Christian soldier and confessor (late 4th century)
person_name_full: Victricius, Christian soldier and confessor (late 4th century)
notes: Christian soldier and confessor in the late 4th century, known for refusing military service on Christian grounds and enduring persecution.
Roman poet known for the Aeneid, influential in Latin literature and Roman cultural identity.
Roman emperor in 69 CE known for his extravagant and indulgent lifestyle, ruled during the Year of the Four Emperors.
Roman friend and companion of Pliny the Younger, served as a flamen (priest) of a province.
Greek philosopher, head of the Academy after Plato, known for his learned and severe character.

person_name: Zeno of Citium (Zeno the Stoic, founder of Stoicism, c. 334–262 BCE)
person_name_full: Zeno of Citium (Zeno the Stoic, founder of Stoicism, c. 334–262 BCE)
notes: Founder of Stoicism, influential Hellenistic philosopher from Citium.
Queen of Palmyra who ruled c. 267–272 CE, known for her leadership and resistance against Roman Emperor Aurelian.
A well-educated freedman known for his skill in comedy, mentioned by Pliny the Younger.
A lanista of Capua.
